key_insight: Elastic surface wrinkling in a compressed hyperelastic half-space is reinterpreted as a topological phase transition with a quantized change in winding number and a localized zero-energy edge state.
engineering_relevance: This reframes soft-matter instability as a controllable, potentially programmable mechanism for smart surfaces and morphing materials under large deformation.
method: Generalize the Stroh formalism with Lie-group metric evolution to encode nonlinear geometric frustration.
Map the surface-impedance matrix to a 1D Dirac Hamiltonian, using mechanical stretch as the tunable mass term.
Identify the wrinkle onset via the topological invariant transition from trivial to non-trivial phase.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports an analytic mapping and theoretical characterization of the instability, but does not mention experimental validation or numerical benchmarking.
watch_item: The topological interpretation depends on idealized hyperelastic half-space assumptions, so real materials with finite thickness, defects, or dissipation may deviate from the predicted invariants.
paper_title: Elastic Surface Instability as a Topological Phase Transition
